Scenario.overview: Communications Industry Complex A sophisticated cyber attack orchestrated by the Maroochy Water Breach group targets the communications sector with the aim of disrupting critical operations. Utilizing basic skills, the attackers employ a multi-stage approach involving reconnaissance, resource development, and various post-compromise techniques to achieve their objectives.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Network Traffic, User Account, Windows Registry, Target Artifact (Mobile device), Application Log, Logon Session, Target Artifact (Channel)
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Bash Script, Process Creation (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Network Service Scanning, Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309; Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map the network topology of the target's communication infrastructure.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522; Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers establish a phishing server or utilize public forums to procure necessary resources and malware for the operation.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2019-1652 (Suggested): Cisco Small Business RV320 and RV325 Dual Gigabit WAN VPN Routers Vulnerability
stage.description: Attackers send crafted emails to employees to gain access to the network via phishing.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2023-29552 (Suggested): Service Location Protocol (SLP) Denial-of-Service Vulnerability
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 88; Name: OS Command Injection
stage.description: Once initial access is achieved, attackers use command-line tools to execute malicious commands.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 70; Name: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: Backdoor accounts are created to maintain persistent access after reboots or logouts.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121; Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Attackers exploit vulnerabilities in applications to escalate privileges.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578; Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: Defensive mechanisms such as antivirus software are turned off to avoid detection.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 49; Name: Password Brute Forcing
stage.description: Repeated attempts are made to guess login credentials.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309; Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Internal network mapping is conducted to identify valuable systems.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used to move laterally across the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277; Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Sensitive data is extracted from repositories like SharePoint or file servers.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24; Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Collected data is transferred to attacker-controlled servers using command and control channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276; Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Operational technology systems are disrupted to cause physical damage or service interruption, mirroring the Maroochy Water Breach.

Scenario.summary: The Maroochy Water Breach group employs a complex multi-stage cyber attack targeting the communications sector. Starting with reconnaissance and resource development, they gain initial access through phishing, execute malicious payloads, establish persistence, and escalate privileges. They evade defenses, access credentials, discover valuable systems, move laterally, collect sensitive data, exfiltrate it over command and control channels, and finally impact the targeted systems, aiming to disrupt operations.
